retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- History and Physical -- Initial Apical Periodontitis
retrieved_docs.content: The early stage of apical periodontitis is an inflammatory process where neutrophils are extravasated to the area through chemotaxis. The neutrophils attack and kill the offending microorganisms and release leukotrienes and prostaglandins, which attract more neutrophils and macrophages to the site. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- History and Physical -- Initial Apical Periodontitis
retrieved_docs.content: Initial apical periodontitis can result from both infectious or aseptic inflammation. [7] It can be caused by microbial invasion into the periapical tissues, trauma, instrumentation injury, endodontic materials, and chemical irritation. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- History and Physical -- Chronic Apical Periodontitis
retrieved_docs.content: If the oral microbes and their by-products remain after the initial host response, the lesion may turn from a neutrophil-dominated lesion to a macrophage-dominated lesion encapsulated in connective tissue. [7] Unlike their acute counterpart, chronic lesions are often asymptomatic. Due to the destruction of the periodontal ligament and the alveolar bone, radiographic changes become evident.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- History and Physical -- Initial Apical Periodontitis
retrieved_docs.content: Activated macrophages release other cytokines that intensify the vascular response and osteoclastic bone resorption. At this point, the acute phase may take different courses: healing or worsening the infection. When root canal therapy is initiated, and the offending microbes are removed from the root canal system, the lesion may resolve, and the periapical tissues will remain unchanged radiographically. [14] Conversely, if the infection is not resolved, it will become chronic, receiving the name of chronic apical periodontitis. [8] These lesions may spread through the bone to other fascial spaces causing abscesses or opening to the exterior, creating a fistula.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Apical diseases can manifest with various clinical presentations, ranging from no obvious clinical signs or symptoms to severe destruction of the underlying bone, with or without a draining abscess. Therefore, proper identification, diagnosis, and treatment of apical diseases can prevent severe clinical signs and symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with diabetes typically have a higher frequency of tooth loss and endodontically treated teeth due to increased chronic inflammation, reduced tissue repair capacity, decreased response to infection, and delayed wound healing. [10] Patients who previously received endodontic therapy also had a higher incidence of AP than those with non-endodontically treated teeth. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A complete medical history should be taken at every patient encounter, including medications and allergies, as antibiotics aid in treating some apical diseases by decreasing the associated complex bacterial load. [12] In addition, a history of the patient's symptoms can help the clinician determine the origin of the patient's chief complaint. Some lesions may be symptomatic, while others may be accidental findings during routine dental care and treatment. The clinician must determine whether the lesions are odontogenic or non-odontogenic in origin through a thorough clinical and radiographic examination. Apical periodontitis can be classified according to its presentation into 1) initial apical periodontitis (also known as acute apical periodontitis) and 2) chronic apical periodontitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Apical periodontitis results from the encounter between microbial organisms and the host defense system. [7] Several factors influence the virulence and pathogenicity of the invading microbes, including the interactions with other microorganisms present in the root canal, the ability to evade and interfere with host cellular defenses, the release of bacterial modulins, and finally, their ability to synthesize enzymes that damage host tissues. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Severe inflammatory reactions can lead to pulpal cell injury, potentially compromising pulpal vitality and survivability. Although the infection activates an immune response, immune cells and molecules are unable to effectively penetrate dentin to remove pathogens due to the narrow apical foramen of the tooth and the hard enamel surrounding the internal structures. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Apical periodontitis is the local inflammation of the periapical tissues that originate from pulp disease. [1] It may occur due to the advancement of dental caries, trauma, or operative dental procedures. [2] The infected pulp is the main cause of apical periodontitis. The host defense response to pathogenic microbes is what triggers inflammation and the consequent destruction of periradicular tissues.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- History and Physical -- Chronic Apical Periodontitis
retrieved_docs.content: In the chronic phase, activated T- cells produce a variety of cytokines that down-regulate and suppress the osteoclastic activity (reducing bone resorption) and increase the production of connective tissue growth factor (TGF-beta). [7] The reduction in bone resorption and the regeneration of connective tissue structure explains why chronic lesions can remain dormant and asymptomatic for extended periods without significant radiographic changes. Histopathologically, chronic apical periodontitis lesions are encapsulated with fibrous tissue, and some may be epithelized. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- History and Physical -- Initial Apical Periodontitis
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms at this stage include pain, tenderness on pressure, difficulty eating in the area, and sensation of tooth elevation. [7] [13] Clinicians will rely on patients reporting their signs and symptoms for diagnosis. Radiographic changes in the periapical tissues are rarely observed in the initial stages of the condition because the inflammation is limited to the periodontal ligament, and the host response has not had enough time for substantial tissue changes to occur. [7] Therefore, bone, cementum, and dentine integrity have not yet been disrupted. [7] It is noteworthy that the lesion may resolve, the apical tissues may restore when the irritant is non-infectious, and no further irritation occurs. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Differential Diagnosis -- Apical Radiopacities
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercementosis Cemento-osseous dysplasia Idiopathic osteosclerosis Apical radiopacities are likely not associated with endodontic infection directly but are commonly found on routine radiographic evaluation. Radiopacities are generally benign.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In acute apical periodontitis, the dental pulp may remain vital or have lost vitality and become necrotic. The tooth will be tender and painful on percussion. Radiographic examination is usually unremarkable, or there may be just a small thickening of the periodontal ligament space and a slight loss of lamina dura in the periapex. By contrast, in chronic apical periodontitis, the pulp is necrotic and infected; therefore, pulp sensibility tests will not elicit a response. The tooth is not tender to palpation, pressure, or percussion, but it may have some mobility and feel different. The finding of a radiolucent lesion in the periapex on x-ray marks the stage of chronic apical periodontitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Thus, we can describe apical periodontitis as an inflammatory disease due to the dynamic interaction between oral bacterial invaders and the body's defensive mechanism at the tooth apex. [7] Despite the complex host defensive system, the causative organisms are well-entrenched in the intricate and delicate root canal system, which is beyond the reach of the host immune response. [8] Therefore, apical periodontitis is not self-healing, and resolution of the disease can only result through surgical or non-surgical endodontic therapy or removal of the tooth in question. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Around 52% of the worldwide adult population has had at least one tooth affected by apical periodontitis (AP). [9] There is a slightly higher prevalence of AP among individuals in developing countries compared to developed ones, likely attributed to access to care and patient education. The frequency of AP was also greater in individuals with systemic diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” serves as a causal indicator and is associated with specific injury outcomes, including loss of teeth resulting from an accident (“verlies; tanden door ongeval”) as well as severe trauma such as crushing injuries or internal injuries. Recognizing this wording helps clinicians link the underlying cause to these particular clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: diabetes mellitus, its specific complications (various forms of retinopathy and nephropathy), and the relationship between these complications and ocular destructive procedures is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1448
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is a clinical symptom that can be associated with تضيق الأبهر (aortic stenosis), a cardiac condition characterized by narrowing of the aortic valve and resulting hemodynamic compromise, as well as with داء المبيضات الفموي (oral thrush), an infection of the oral mucosa caused by Candida species. While تضيق الأبهر represents a direct cardiovascular cause of chest discomfort, داء المبيضات الفموي may reflect systemic or immunologic factors that can also manifest with chest pain, highlighting that nonspecific chest pain may arise from both cardiac and non‑cardiac origins. Recognizing this dual association is important for clinicians to conduct a thorough evaluation and to tailor management strategies appropriately.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1923
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب العصيدي is a medical condition that is associated with ذَبْهَةُ برنزميتال. Clinically, التصلب العصيدي is linked to several findings, including النفخة الانقباضية, حفظ الصحة الروتيني, عرج, and لغط الشريان السباتي. In addition, التصلب العصيدي has a recognized subtype called التصلب العصيدي المعمم, reflecting a more generalized form of the disease. These associations are important for recognizing the clinical presentation of التصلب العصيدي and guiding appropriate evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 210
retrieved_docs.content: modalities, procedural interventions, and disease processes are anatomically and clinically related, providing a comprehensive framework for the evaluation and management of abdominal, hepatic, and biliary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as being due to loss of teeth caused by an accident, indicating that dental injury resulting from an accident can lead to unintended physical contact.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 0 -- Community 25
retrieved_docs.content: is contraindicated in pancreatitis, and that pancreatic disorders unrelated to diabetes are mapped to pancreatitis. Pancreatitis is associated with increased systemic and tissue alkalinity, a factor that may affect diagnostic interpretation and therapeutic choices.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is clinically associated with nonspecific chest pain and with fatigue (التَّعَب). These findings indicate that patients with oral candidiasis may present not only with typical oral lesions but also with systemic or atypical symptoms such as unexplained chest discomfort and generalized tiredness, underscoring the importance of considering this fungal infection in the differential diagnosis when such nonspecific complaints arise.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1238
retrieved_docs.content: malformation of the choroid (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), partial perimacular choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي). Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroid is a central site for diverse pathological processes, and recognizing the specific type of choroidal involvement—whether inflammatory, scar‑forming, degenerative, traumatic, metastatic, or congenital—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2768
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain, a symptom that lacks a clearly defined cause, has been reported to be clinically associated with oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي). This relationship suggests that patients who present with unexplained chest discomfort may warrant evaluation for an underlying fungal infection of the oral cavity, particularly when other risk factors for candidiasis are present. Recognizing this association can help clinicians adopt a more comprehensive diagnostic approach and ensure appropriate treatment for both the chest pain and any concurrent oral candidiasis.
